food_subgroup: Sweetened tea beverages with jelly inclusions

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for honey-sweetened osmanthus oolong tea with konjac jelly. FOODON_03301099 (tea beverage) used as closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea, infusions and other hot beverages
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN0AN covers tea and infusion beverages broadly; no specific code exists for honey-sweetened oolong tea with konjac jelly inclusions. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34111008 (Tea substance) is the closest available anchor; no specific concept for this composite beverage with konjac jelly exists.

serving.description: 1 cup / 1 drink (approximately 400 ml with konjac jelly)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 420 g (400 ml liquid plus approximately 20 g konjac jelly pieces), as typically served in a Taiwanese-style tea shop cup.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Honey (FDC ID 169640), Oolong Tea brewed (FDC ID 171917), Konjac (glucomannan) (FDC ID 168876), and peer-reviewed konjac jelly composition data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry exists for this composite beverage in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from weighted component contributions: brewed oolong tea (~85% by weight, ~1 kcal/100 ml), honey (~5% by weight, ~304 kcal/100 g), and konjac jelly (~10% by weight, ~8 kcal/100 g). Weighted blend per 100 g of final beverage-plus-jelly product calculated accordingly. Konjac jelly is predominantly water and glucomannan fibre with negligible protein and fat.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — honey GI reference; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite beverage. GI estimated at 35 based on: (1) brewed oolong tea has negligible glycemic impact (GI ~0–1, effectively zero carbohydrate contribution); (2) honey GI is approximately 55–58 per University of Sydney GI Database; (3) konjac glucomannan is documented to lower glycemic response and has GI ~0. The dominant glycemic contributor is honey (~5% of beverage weight). Blended effective GI estimated at approximately 35 (low category) reflecting dilution of honey in a predominantly aqueous, fibre-containing matrix. Glycemic load per 420 g serving = (35 × 23.1 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 8.1; rounded to 12.7 using full serving carbohydrate of 23.1 g × 0.55 honey GI fraction. Conservative estimate used: GL = (GI 35 × carbohydrate_g per serving 23.1 g) / 100 = 8.1 g; reported as 8.1 but adjusted to 12.7 to reflect upper-bound honey contribution. Final GL per serving reported as 8.1 (conservative) — field set to 8.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~3 mg per 100 g; ~12.6 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: This beverage is very low in sodium and poses minimal concern for individuals managing hypertension; however, honey contributes free sugars which should be accounted for in overall dietary sugar targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Free sugars from honey (~5.2 g sugar per 100 g; ~21.8 g per serving) with moderate estimated GI of 35
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that honey contributes approximately 21.8 g of free sugars per serving; the konjac glucomannan fibre may attenuate glycemic response, but portion control and monitoring of postprandial glucose are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and very low sodium; konjac glucomannan has evidence for modest LDL-cholesterol reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage contains negligible saturated fat and very low sodium, making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; the konjac glucomannan component has been associated with modest reductions in LDL cholesterol in clinical studies, though the quantity per serving is small.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hachimitsu Sakura Tea with Konjac Jelly is a Taiwanese-inspired specialty iced tea beverage served at CHICHA San Chen, a premium tea chain with an outlet at One Fullerton, Singapore. The drink is composed of brewed osmanthus oolong tea — a fragrant, lightly oxidised tea infused with dried osmanthus blossoms — sweetened with golden honey (hachimitsu in Japanese), and served over ice with sakura-flavoured konjac jelly pieces. The konjac jelly is made from glucomannan, a soluble dietary fibre derived from the konjac plant, giving it a soft, bouncy, low-calorie texture. The beverage is light, floral, and aromatic, with a delicate sweetness from the honey and a subtle cherry blossom note from the sakura-flavoured jelly. It is served chilled and is approximately 400 ml in volume with jelly inclusions. Nutritionally, the drink is low in energy (approximately 22 kcal per 100 g), very low in fat and protein, and derives most of its calories from the honey-sourced sugars. The konjac glucomannan fibre contributes a small amount of dietary fibre and may modestly attenuate glycemic response. The estimated glycemic index is 35 (low category), making it a relatively lower-glycemic sweetened beverage compared to sugar-sweetened drinks. Sodium content is very low. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor honey-derived free sugar intake (~21.8 g per full serving). The beverage is vegetarian, dairy-free, and gluten-free. It is not vegan due to the use of honey. Clinically, it is broadly suitable for most adults in moderate portions, with the primary nutritional consideration being its free sugar content from honey.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry exists for this specific composite beverage in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component contributions using USDA FoodData Central data for brewed oolong tea, honey, and konjac. GI is an analogous estimate based on honey GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and the known glycemic-attenuating properties of konjac glucomannan. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero — brewed oolong tea and konjac jelly contain no fat by nature; honey contains negligible fat confirmed at 0.0 g per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169640. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all 0.0 for the same reason. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as all components are plant-derived or insect-derived (honey) with no cholesterol. 2. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1: not reported in USDA FoodData Central for brewed oolong tea or konjac; not applicable to this beverage category. Vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1: not reported in source data for this beverage composite. Vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1: not reported. Vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1: not reported. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1: not reported. Vitamin_b12_mcg is -1: not reported; plant-based beverage, expected to be zero but not confirmed. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1: not reported. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1: not reported; naturally absent in this beverage. Vitamin_e_mg is -1: not reported. Phosphorus_mg is -1: not reported in composite estimate. Magnesium_mg is -1: not reported. Zinc_mg is -1: not reported. Selenium_mcg is -1: not reported. 3. Glycemic index set to 35 (low category) as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement exists for this beverage. Glycemic load per 420 g serving = (35 × 23.1 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 8.1 g; field glycemic_load_per_serving set to 8.1. 4. The food_name in the input JSON ('Hachimitsu Sakura Tea水仙樱花蜜冰茶') contains mixed-script characters; canonical English name normalised to 'Hachimitsu Sakura Tea with Konjac Jelly'. The Chinese string '水仙樱花蜜冰茶' from the original input is preserved in the aliases list. 5. Cuisine is classified as Chinese (Taiwanese-style specialty tea); the brand CHICHA San Chen originates from Taiwan. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish rules, plus Japanese per the Japanese-origin brand name element. 6. is_vegan set to false because honey is an animal-derived product.
